Partials

Partials are reusable template fragments. They let you define your header, navigation, and footer once and include them on every page — keeping your templates DRY and easy to maintain.

What partials are

A partial is an .html template file in the partials/ subdirectory at your site root. You include it in any other template using the <outpost-include partial="name" /> tag. The included content is compiled and rendered inline, exactly as if you had written it directly in the parent template.

Partials are ideal for any HTML structure that appears on multiple pages: the site header, the navigation bar, the footer, a sidebar, a card component, or a cookie banner. Instead of copying the same markup into every template, you put it in a partial once and include it wherever needed.

Where partials live

Partials live in the partials/ directory at your site root — the same directory that contains your page templates and the outpost/ folder:

my-site/
  index.html
  about.html
  blog.html
  partials/
    head.html
    nav.html
    footer.html
    components/
      card.html
      hero.html
  outpost/

Include syntax

Reference partials by name, without the .html extension and without the partials/ path prefix:

<outpost-include partial="head"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="nav"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="footer"></outpost-include>

These three lines correspond to the files:

partials/head.html
partials/nav.html
partials/footer.html

Subdirectory partials

You can organize partials into subdirectories. Use a forward slash in the include name to reference them:

<outpost-include partial="components/card"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="components/hero"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="sections/testimonials"></outpost-include>

These resolve to:

partials/components/card.html
partials/components/hero.html
partials/sections/testimonials.html

Standard partials

By convention, most Outpost sites use three standard partials:

PartialPurpose
head.htmlDOCTYPE through opening <body> tag — meta tags, CSS links, favicon, analytics scripts
nav.htmlSite navigation — logo, menu, mobile toggle
footer.htmlFooter content, closing </body></html>, JS includes

These names are not enforced — you can name your partials anything. But Forge (the AI site builder) recognizes these standard names when auto-detecting site structure.

Tip — Place global fields (data-scope="global") in your partials so they are available on every page. Site name in nav.html, analytics ID in head.html, footer text in footer.html — they all scaffold into Admin → Globals automatically.
